pages.name: The One-Stop Spot For The Enterprising Edgerunner

text.content:
This collection of services is unlocked once Robbin The Hood has been completed.

Cost

1 eagle opens the door. This fee is mandatory. The doors stay open for any parties the buyer allows in, but only the buyer gets the Finer Things and Local Expertise bonuses. Multiple players can choose to make the purchase, though.

Note: this fee can be waived for those entering the bar on the Afterlife's request, but freeloaders won't get the Finer Things or Local Expertise bonus.

Function

The bar provides the following services:

  • The Finer Things: Anyone who pays the entry cost of the bar regains up to 1d3 humanity (after spending some time there, reflecting their splurge on the nicer things in life.) All their drinks at the club are comped as part of the entry fee.

    • Freeloaders don't get these perks. Don't be stingy - spend for yourself!

  • Complete Security: The bar's back rooms are known to be completely secure - what's said inside won't ever leave, unless someone present for the conversation spills the beans.

    • This is, at the moment, the only area in which plans can be concocted and false identities constructed which are immune from the Arasaka Corp-Wide Ability, Arasaka Counterintelligence. This immunity is lost once the information is disclosed outside that context.

  • Rubbing Elbows/Edgerunners For Hire: The Afterlife always has a few unusual characters in it, to build out the edgerunning community. See Extra Details - Generating Bar Occupants for more.

  • Local Expertise: Need to find something? The bartender knows a guy - they might even be at the bar right now! Characters may substitute their Local Expert skill for Local Expert (All Of Night City) while in the bar, and receive a +2 to the check.

    • Note: this assumes the bartender has been given enough info to know what the player is looking for.

    • At GM discretion, a suitable individual might already be in the bar, if the check is passed.

      • This is one of the most popular places in the city to rub elbows, after all.

    • If the located whatever-it-is involves buying a product or service that can't just be found at a Bodega, that purchase is discounted by 10%, after any barter/trading checks have been made (assuming they are).

      • Everyone wants a good word about them at the Afterlife!

  • Casino: The Afterlife's gambling den minigame is also located here, though its features won't be unlocked until a later mission.

  • Bounties: The bar staff maintain an active bounty board, which is the primary way to earn Eagles, once the established missions in this pack have been completed. See Extra Details for more.

    • Optionally, bounties can be rolled as side objectives while on a job, with their associated rewards.

The Afterlife attracts all kinds of colorful characters, each one with a unique bonus. Each time the party visits the club for the first time in the day and inquires after the inhabitants, the bartender can introduce them. The following edgerunner services are always available:

  • (Solo) Dirty Deeds, Done Dirt Cheap: Various solos from around the city are present in the bar, available to hire (payment is per beat where they must fight). One or more of the following can be chosen:

    • Afterlife Solo/RatSec Elite (2 Eagles per beat of combat)

    • Afterlife Edgerunner/6th Street Lieutenant (4 Eagles per)

    • Afterlife Hitman/Iron Sights Hitman (5 Eagles per).

    • The paying player gains control of the NPC while they're in combat, but they will flee if seriously wounded.

  • (Rockerboy) Birthday Party Rockstar: A rockerboy will play a show as a distraction at a requested location.

    • Costs 1 eagle per rank in rockerboy that'd be required to play the location.

    • The fee is doubled if a fight breaks out that threatens the rockerboy or their fans (the Afterlife enforces this rule).

    • Uses Afterlife Rockerboy/Vampyre Performer statblock, and gains the full amount of luck from its own ability.

    • The GM retains control of the rockerboy throughout the performance.

  • (Tech) Bubblegum & Scotch Tape: A tech will jerryrig a tech upgrade to an item the party owns.

    • Lasts 24 hours, takes 10 minutes.

      • Costs 1 Eagle for a 100eb item, 3 for a 500eb item, and 5 for anything beyond that.

  • (Media) Targeted Advertising: A media will generate one rumor which everyone in the next gig will believe to be the truth, except for the players and the media. Only those directly involved in the gig will believe it to be true.

    • The rumor cannot be used to score a rep bonus/complete a bonus objective/defeat an enemy/make a friend.

    • The work costs 1 Eagle per rep, for the highest rep enemy in the job.

    • For every additional fact required to sell the lie, the media charges the same price again.

  • (Fixer) Can He Fix It?: A fixer will provide the paying player with one key piece of information about the upcoming job - how many enemies are present, what kind of weapons they're bringing, a rough layout of a building, etc.

    • The rumor cannot be used to score a rep bonus/complete a bonus objective/defeat an enemy/make a friend.

    • The work costs 1/2 an Eagle per rep (rounded up), for the highest rep enemy in the job.

    • For each additional piece of information, the player is charged again.

  • (Lawman) I Am The Law: A Lawman and his posse will be on site to arrest and detain any number of non-essential mooks, before any fighting can break out.

    • The mooks will be released after 4 hours with their gear.

    • Costs 1 Eagle for every rep point that a captured mook is expected to have

    • This feature cannot capture any mooks with 4 or more rep.

  • (Exec) Executive Privilege: An exec will use their connections to stop the NCPD or another relevant corp from interfering on the job, assuming the job doesn't present the NCPD/the corp in question as a direct opponent.

    • Costs 2 Eagles per player character on the job.

    • Lasts for exactly 1 hour after the NCPD or other forces would otherwise be summoned; after the hour elapses, the forces arrive as normal.

    • For each additional hour's stay, the initial fee is doubled.

  • (Medtech) Kirkland Brand Trauma Team: A medtech will be waiting in the wings to stabilize and perform surgery on any players which require it.

    • Uses the Afterlife Medtech/Trauma Team Doctor statblock, without the latter half of the Trauma Team Corp-Wide Ability involving ceasefires.

    • Costs 1 Eagle per total average party role rank (minimum 4).

  • (Nomad) Getaway Driver: A nomad will wait on standby with a vehicle, ready to peel out with whatever the players need transported.

    • Uses the Afterlife Driver/Steel Vaquero Driver statblock.

    • The driver will wait at a predetermined location, and travel to either a predetermined final location or a predetermined fallback point with no stops in-between.

      • All locations are determined at the point of sale.

      • The driver will fight in self-defense but will not actively attack an enemy.

    • Costs 1 Eagle per seat needed to the party (minimum 2), with the vehicle being changed accordingly, as follows:

      • 2-4 seats: Buggy or Generic Compact with seating upgrades as-needed.

      • 5-8 seats: Generic Pickup or Generic Van, with seating upgrades as-needed.

      • 9+ seats: Generic bus.

    • This price is doubled for sea transport, and tripled for air transport.

      • For sea transport, a speedboat is used, with associated seating upgrades as needed.

      • For air transport, a Generic A/V-4 is used, with associated seating upgrades as needed.

    • 100lbs/50kgs of excess material/trunk space = 1 seat.

    • If additional transport is needed, or if players prefer a larger array of smaller vehicles, multiple nomads can be recruited for the same price.

  • Slicing and Dicing: A netrunner will seize control of one system on the jobsite via a virus that can be inserted through a memory shard at any terminal, guaranteed to pass one check.

    • Costs 1 Eagle per DV bracket of the check (see Casual Netrunning under Part 0 for examples).

    • Multiple shards can be purchased.

    • Once the shard is removed, the system reverts to its pre-hack state.

Meet Your New Friends

Any time an edgerunner is recruited, oll the following tables.

  1. Origin: Determines their background.

  2. Nationality: Night City is a cultural hub, attracting people from all over the world.

  3. Personality: Determines their mannerisms and speaking habits.

I get it - nobody likes radiant, procedurally generated content. And yet, random encounters provide the lifeblood of many TTRPGs. This expands on that - the bounty board provides the essential function of giving you short-notice material that works in the context of the game, and also provides optional side content for players to explore while doing the main missions, just to make things a little more interesting.

By nature of the beast, these tables will require a fair amount of improvisation from the GM. Unfortunately, there isn't much way around this.

To begin:

  1. Find a map, or else decide to include the bounty as side content on another mission which has room to support it.

    • Looking for maps? The cyberpunk red subreddit is a good source. I also recommend MidJourney, if you need something quick and generic. A prompt of "A Top Down Battlemap of X" followed by the tags "Cyberpunk theme, intricate detail, volumetric and atmospheric lighting, detailed background, unreal engine 5," and a pair of colors often produces reasonably coherent, albeit somewhat jumbled results.

  2. Roll the employers table and determine who's offering the job.

  3. Roll the job table and determine what's at stake.

  4. Roll the rewards table to see what the payout for the job is.

Regarding enemy distribution and difficulty…

I'd like to say that I have a magic algorithm that spits out the perfect difficulty for the game, based on the rewards offered. But the truth is, enemies are too variable for that - Militech and Maelstrom are a few steps above the gonks in the Vampyres, and they're themselves a step above the generic gangoons/RatSec forces.

So… exercise your best judgement. The payout table defines 5 tiers. Here's my suggestions for them.

Tier 5 - Basic: Should not be a threat to a prepared party, though might be difficult if only 1-2 individuals were involved. DV for checks should be 15 at most. 2077 Parallel: Flaming Crotch Man.

Tier 4 - Night City Life: Will pose a moderate challenge to a well prepared party, with elements of lethality if things go wrong. DV for checks should hover around 13-17. 2077 Parallel: Retrieving Sandra Dorsett

Tier 3 - Risky Biscuits: Will pose a substantial threat, even to a prepared party, and things very well might not go according to plan. Death is certainly a possibility. Check DVs should hover between 15-21. 2077 Parallel: Retrieving the spiderbot from Maelstrom.

Tier 2 - Variable: Depending on the value of the item in question, might be basic, might be major leagues. Up to you.

Tier 1 - Major Leagues: You'll need a solid plan, perfect intel and a good group of people, to even consider this, and it may end up paying out more than anything else in the bar, at the moment. Expect a serious investment, for a nice chunk of reward. Check DVs should average 17+. 2077 Parallel: stealing from Konpeki Plaza.
